title: HTTP APIs and Channels
description: Run a real node:http server as a managed resource, sliced from the page-writer example.
channels_desc: A channel is a component that owns a network endpoint. It starts a server when its handler runs, feeds incoming requests into the reactive system through callback props, and closes the socket on cleanup.
server_intro: The page-writer's <0>HttpChannel</0> wraps a <1>node:http</1> server in useAsyncOutput, so the listening socket has the same lifecycle as any other managed resource:
server_notice: The handler resolves only after <0>listen</0> succeeds, publishes the URL with <1>setOutputs</1>, and returns a cleanup function. Requests never touch the runtime directly — they call the callback props the parent passed in.
routes_intro: Routing is plain Node.js code. Each route validates its input and delegates to a prop like <0>onCreatePage</0> or <1>onListPages</1>:
routes_notice: The channel stays generic — it knows HTTP, not pages. <0>onCreatePage</0> updates a store in the parent, new Page resources mount, and the response returns immediately with the accepted slug.
teardown_intro: Two small helpers finish the component: a JSON responder and a promise-wrapped <0>server.close</0> used by the cleanup function:
info_cleanup: The cleanup returned from the handler releases the port when the component unmounts or before the handler re-runs, so <0>--watch</0> restarts never hit an address-in-use error.
see_walkthrough: See the whole request-to-page pipeline in the <0>page-writer walkthrough</0>.
